raw_case:
Page kidney in a solid-organ transplant recipient with acute renal failure and arterial hypertension


Summary

Page kidney (Page phenomenon) is a rare and treatable cause of hypertension which can occur if a haematoma, leading to extrinsic compression, occurs after renal transplant biopsy. The renin–angiotensin–aldosterone system is activated leading to hypertension. This case report is that of a male in his 70s who had a renal transplant biopsy of his transplanted kidney and subsequently developed Page phenomenon as a result of an expanding haematoma. He developed elevated blood pressure (BP) along with a decrease in his renal function. Surgical intervention was used to salvage his transplanted kidney. The index case highlights the importance of being aware of the signs of Page kidney to ensure early recognition and intervention. To our knowledge, this is the first case of a Page kidney in a renal allograft documented in the Caribbean region.

Background

 Page kidney/Page phenomenon is a condition that is caused by the external compression of the kidney and renal parenchyma, which results in activation of the renin–angiotensin–aldosterone system (RAAS) leading to hypertension and can cause decline in kidney function.1Page kidney was first described in 1939 by Irvine Page who conducted experiments where he wrapped canine kidneys in cellophane which resulted in the inflammatory response and production of a fibrocollagenous shell that compressed the kidney and small parenchymal vessels, leading to ischemia, activation of RAAS and ultimately hypertension.1There have been many aetiologies described for Page kidney including blunt trauma, spontaneous and iatrogenic. This case report will focus on the management of Page kidney in a male who underwent an ultrasound-guided biopsy of his transplanted kidney.

Case presentation

The patient was a male in his 70s with a history of well-controlled hypertension for approximately 48 years, diabetes mellitus type 2, orthostatic proteinuria and ischaemic heart disease. In the late ‘90s, he was diagnosed with stage 2 right renal cell carcinoma for which he had a right nephrectomy. He subsequently developed hypertensive renal disease which eventually caused end-stage renal disease. The patient received a living donor kidney transplant approximately 12 years after his nephrectomy; the surgery was uneventful. Also in his surgical history was a right hemithyroidectomy for a cold thyroid nodule and parathyroidectomy for a parathyroid adenoma. His immunosuppressive medications were mycophenolic acid, cyclosporine and prednisolone. His other medications were valsartan, bisoprolol, amlodipine, terazosin, linagliptin, rosuvastatin, allopurinol, aspirin and thyroxine. His post-transplant kidney function showed creatinine levels of 97–143 µmol/L (normal range 68–126 µmol/L).12 years after his renal transplant, he was noted to have worsening proteinuria thought to be due to chronic rejection or transplant glomerulopathy. As such, he was referred for an ultrasound-guided renal transplant biopsy by an interventional radiologist at an ambulatory radiology facility. Preprocedure laboratory investigations showed a haemoglobin (Hb) of 120 g/L (normal range 130–180 g/L), urea of 9 mmol/L (normal range 2.6–6.5 mmol /L) and creatinine of 145 µmol/L. Of note, the patient was on aspirin and had taken a dose the day before the biopsy. After the biopsy, the interventional radiologist noted ongoing bleeding through the biopsy needle and was concerned that the bleeding was not stopping. The patient was given desmopressin and tranexamic acid at the time and approximately 1 hour later had an ultrasound which revealed a perinephric haematoma. Due to concerns of ongoing bleeding, he was referred and subsequently transported to the tertiary-level healthcare facility for observation and multidisciplinary management by the renal transplant surgeon and the transplant nephrologist in the intensive care unit.On admission, he was haemodynamically stable with a BP of 139/67 mm Hg, the biopsy needle was still in situ, and on removal of the stylet, there was no bleeding noted and the needle was removed. He was kept fasted for the possibility of proceeding to surgical evacuation of the haematoma. Admission investigations revealed an Hb of 114 g/L, platelets of 260 K/µL, (normal range 150–450 K/µL), urea of 12.8 mmol/L and creatinine of 183 µmol/L. Approximately 8 hours post biopsy, he had a repeat ultrasound, which showed that the perinephric haematoma had increased in size. At that time, the decision was made, as the patient was stable (normotensive), to continue to monitor vitals, blood investigations and urine output which was normal at that time.However, over the next 8 hours, the patient’s urine output decreased notably to only 80 mL despite continuous fluid boluses with crystalloids. His BP increased to a maximum of 172/80 mm Hg. Repeat Hb was 105 g/L, platelets were 233 K/µL, urea was 14.9 mmol/L and creatinine rose to 272 µmol/L. A clinical diagnosis of Page kidney was made given the worsening creatinine levels, the fall in urine output, despite boluses, and the rising BP. A multidisciplinary decision (by renal transplant surgery, nephrology, intensive care and radiology) following review of the patient was made to carry out surgical intervention due to concern that the haematoma was expanding further from ongoing bleeding within the tight capsule. Given that the patient was on aspirin at the time of the biopsy, there was also the concern that he was not clotting adequately. The fact that this was the patient’s only functioning kidney made salvaging it even more critical. The patient was consented for capsulotomy of the transplant kidney and evacuation of the haematoma. Blood tests done preoperation showed Hb of 97 g/L, platelets of 221 K/µL, urea of 16.6 mmol/L and creatinine of 321 µmol/L.The patient improved during the postoperative period. Immediately, his urine output normalised to a rate of 0.5–1 mL/kg/hour. Post surgery, he required labetalol 2.5 mg intravenously two times and was recommenced on his amlodipine 10 mg and bisoprolol 5 mg orally. His postoperative Hb was 7.9 mmol/L, platelets were 211 K/µL, urea was 18.4 mmol/L and creatinine was 327 µmol/L. On day 1 postoperation, he was recommenced on terasozin.Closed sterile collection drainage was minimal, initially 54mL/day to 34 mL/day and was removed on day 3 postoperation. He was discharged on day 3 postoperation, with a Hb of 70 g/dL, platelets of 201 K/µL, urea of 19.3 mmol/L and creatinine of 287 µmol/L. His BP prior to discharge had normalised to 139/64 mm Hg, with the remainder of his vitals being within normal range. Discharge planning consisted of close follow-up with the nephrology clinic. The patient’s creatinine level approximately 1 month later was 171 µmol/L; see figures1  2 showing the trend in the creatinine levels. At this time, his Hb was 93 g/L and his urea was 9.8 mmol/L. The biopsy report noted that the specimen was inadequate for diagnosis; there was medulla only which showed medullary peritubular capillaritis which could be from antibody-mediated rejection or be non-specific. A repeat biopsy was recommended, but the patient declined as he was too distressed by the experience. His proteinuria decreased but has not resolved completely.

Investigations

See figures1  2 submitted separately.

Treatment

At surgery, the previous Gibson incision was reopened and the abdominal wall was opened down to the capsule of the transplanted kidney. Findings were that of old clot surrounding the right renal transplant kidney with an approximate 4 cm tangential laceration in the lower half of the kidney with active bleeding at the posterior aspect. The laceration was closed with liver suture. Topical haemostatics (fibrin sealant, oxidised regenerated cellulose and porcine skin gelatin) were used to control residual bleeding. A 19 F Hemovac drain was placed, the external oblique muscle was closed followed by Scarpa’s fascia and the Gibson incision.

Outcome and follow-up

19 months after procedure, the patient has recovered fully without any lasting sequelae, and he carries out his activities of daily living without assistance as he did before the procedure. He remains under multidisciplinary ambulatory care by the renal transplant service. His postprocedure biochemical indices are within the preprocedure range with adequate urine output. His BP control remains stable.

Discussion

Page kidney was first documented in 1955, in a late adolescent male footballer who had a history of blunt trauma 2 years prior, developed hypertension, was found to have a subcapsular haematoma and the hypertension resolved post nephrectomy.2With external compression of the kidney, as seen in that of Page kidney, the intrarenal vessels are affected and this leads to ischaemia and activation of the RAAS, ultimately resulting in elevated BP/hypertension.1Recognising the development of Page kidney is of vital importance to prevent complications. The signs and symptoms may be subtle or severe with the development of hypertensive urgency or emergency.3 It is also possible to have impaired or worsening renal function1 in patients with one kidney as seen in this patient with a transplanted kidney. Diagnosis requires thorough history, examination and imaging findings of a fibrous capsule or haematoma compressing the kidney.4 Most often, Page kidney is diagnosed via imaging modalities, ultrasound scans and CT, which has the added benefit of being able to detect smaller haematomas.5Even though the index case was known to be hypertensive, he presented with a normal BP which rose as his renal function declined which was believed to be as a result of extrinsic compression as described with the Page phenomenon. The immediate resolution of his oliguria and decrease in his creatinine support the diagnosis.As there is no standardised method of treatment for Page kidney, both medical and surgical methods have been employed. The aim of treatment is relieving the compression on the kidney, thereby the ischaemia, and correcting the BP. In the index case, the patient’s BP control was also achieved with medications but it was the relief of the compression on the kidney that led to the return of his renal function.Medical management involves the use of RAAS blockers, which can be used when the compression is resolving spontaneously or following intervention.1 Interventions include both percutaneous methods with image-guided drainage of the haematoma and open evacuation of the haematoma, capsulotomy and nephrectomy. Open evacuation and capsulotomy were chosen in the index case as the haematoma was thought to be expanding from an ongoing bleeding point which required control with suturing and topical haemostatics. An alternative approach could have been the use of angioembolisation to arrest ongoing bleeding with percutaneous drainage of the haematoma; however, angioembolisation is not readily available at our institution.Babel et al6 stated that the major factor influencing treatment modality is the age of the haematoma. Haematomas ≤3 weeks usually resolve and can be monitored while treating BP medically, with the exception that if the BP remains uncontrolled despite medical management, then drainage is required. Haematomas ≥3 weeks require drainage due to formation of pseudocapsules and thus require surgical intervention with capsulotomy or nephrectomy. Of note, however, the haematoma in this patient was thought to be expanding due to ongoing bleeding placing the single-functioning transplanted kidney at risk as seen by the sudden fall in urine output despite boluses of crystalloid and colloid; hence urgent decompression was needed.As seen in this case, complications of renal biopsy in transplant patients will usually occur within the first 24 hours according to a study performed by Morgan et al.7 Major complication of transplant biopsy was defined as needing additional hospitalisation and/or active intervention. They also noted that those major complications occurred in 47 of 2514 biopsies, of which surgical exploration for hematoma evacuation accounted for 15 of the 2514. The study showed a major complication rate of 1.9% for renal transplant biopsy. Of note, transplanted kidneys are known to have a higher percentage of fibrosis and as such have a higher risk of haematoma formation post biopsy.8 Further investigation into the prevalence of Page kidney in transplanted versus native kidneys could yield important findings.Currently, there is no national policy with guidelines governing renal biopsies specifically addressing the cessation of antithrombotic therapy prior to the renal biopsy or the use of specific needles and current practice is physician specific. It would be ideal to have discussions to advance a national policy to minimise complications surrounding this procedure.Despite the low rate of complications after renal transplant biopsy, allograft dysfunction; (temporary or permanent), or loss as a result of Page kidney may occur if diagnosis is delayed and intervention is not implemented early.9 In the case of our patient, the diagnosis was made within 16 hours of admission, and we saw improvement in his renal function prior to discharge with continued improvement post discharge. His current urea is 8.8 mmol/L and his creatinine is 220 µmol/L.

Learning points

Page kidney is a rare phenomenon with no standardised method of treatment.Key clinical findings include elevated blood pressure and renal impairment.Treatment modalities range from conservative medical to invasive surgical procedures.Prompt recognition, diagnosis and intervention are paramount to preventing complications.Urgent surgical intervention can prevent loss of a transplanted kidney with Page phenomenon.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** Male, in his 70s.
- **Chief Complaint:** Acute kidney injury, elevated blood pressure following a renal transplant biopsy.
- **History of Present Illness:** Development of renal dysfunction after undergoing an ultrasound-guided biopsy of his transplanted kidney. Post-biopsy, the patient developed a perinephric hematoma, resulting in declining urine output, deteriorating renal function, and rising blood pressure. 
- **Past Medical History:** Longstanding hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, stage 2 renal cell carcinoma (treated with right nephrectomy), hypertensive renal disease leading to end-stage renal disease, living donor kidney transplant (~12 years earlier), orthostatic proteinuria, ischemic heart disease. Surgeries include right hemithyroidectomy and parathyroidectomy for cold thyroid nodule and parathyroid adenoma, respectively. 
- **Personal History:** On immunosuppressants (mycophenolic acid, cyclosporine, prednisolone) and other medications (valsartan, bisoprolol, amlodipine, terazosin, linagliptin, rosuvastatin, allopurinol, aspirin, thyroxine).
- **Family History:** None mentioned.
- **Physical Examination:** Initially stable on admission with BP 139/67 mmHg; BP later elevated to 172/80 mmHg with worsening renal function and oliguria.
- **Ancillary Tests:** 
  - Pre-procedure: Creatinine 145 µmol/L.
  - Post-biopsy: Rising creatinine to 321 µmol/L, decreasing hemoglobin to 97 g/L, oliguria (~80 mL in 8 hours). 
  - Perinephric hematoma confirmed on ultrasound, which expanded over time.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Acute kidney injury (AKI):**
   - Rationale: The patient developed rising creatinine levels and reduced urine output following a procedure.
   - Exclusion of other causes: No signs of prerenal azotemia, obstruction, or intrinsic renal disease. Symptoms were attributed to external renal compression.

2. **Page kidney:**
   - Rationale: Formation of an expanding perinephric hematoma induced extrinsic compression of the transplanted kidney, leading to activation of the RAAS, hypertension, and worsening kidney function. Oliguria and elevated BP further supported this diagnosis. Imaging confirmed the presence of a hematoma.

3. **Chronic rejection or transplant glomerulopathy:**
   - Considered but excluded due to the temporal relationship between the biopsy and acute symptom onset, suggestive of a mechanical cause (hematoma) rather than chronic immunologic rejection.

4. **Bleeding diathesis due to aspirin use:** 
   - Possible contributing factor given ongoing bleeding post-biopsy.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: The patient was diagnosed with **Page kidney induced by a perinephric hematoma after transplant biopsy**, leading to acute kidney injury and arterial hypertension. The condition required urgent surgical decompression to relieve renal compression and restore kidney function, with subsequent improvement in clinical and biochemical parameters.
generate_case.diagnosis_results: Page kidney due to perinephric hematoma.

results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient is a 70-year-old male with a history of kidney transplant who presented with acute kidney injury (AKI) and elevated blood pressure following a renal transplant biopsy. This temporal relationship strongly suggests that the biopsy procedure is the inciting event for the current acute deterioration.

<step 2>  Post-biopsy, the patient developed a perinephric hematoma, confirmed by ultrasound. This hematoma is a significant complication of renal biopsy. Perinephric hematomas can cause AKI through several mechanisms, including direct compression of the renal parenchyma, compression of the renal vasculature leading to reduced renal blood flow and ischemia, and obstruction of the collecting system if large enough (though less likely in this context based on description, more likely due to compression).

<step 3>  The patient's clinical course is consistent with a perinephric hematoma causing AKI.  He developed oliguria (reduced urine output), a rapid rise in serum creatinine (indicating worsening renal function), and a decrease in hemoglobin (consistent with blood loss into the hematoma). The hematoma was also noted to be expanding, further exacerbating the renal insult.

<step 4> The elevated blood pressure post-biopsy is also likely related to the acute kidney injury and the perinephric hematoma.  Reduced renal perfusion due to compression from the hematoma can activate the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system (RAAS), leading to sodium and water retention and increased blood pressure.  Pre-existing hypertension makes him more susceptible to blood pressure fluctuations.

<step 5>  While the patient has a complex past medical history including hypertension, diabetes, and previous renal cell carcinoma and hypertensive nephropathy leading to ESRD, these are chronic conditions. The acute nature of the presentation, directly following the renal biopsy and coinciding with hematoma formation, points overwhelmingly to a post-biopsy complication as the primary diagnosis for the current AKI.  Immunosuppression regimen is important in transplant patients, but less likely to be the direct cause of this acute event compared to the procedural complication.

<step 6> Considering the clinical picture, the most likely diagnosis is acute kidney injury secondary to perinephric hematoma post-renal transplant biopsy.  While other causes of AKI in a transplant recipient should be considered in a broader differential (like rejection, infection, drug toxicity), the acute onset immediately following the biopsy, the presence of a hematoma, and the supportive clinical and laboratory findings strongly favor this diagnosis. Other less likely possibilities, such as renal artery thrombosis or severe vascular injury during biopsy, could theoretically contribute but are less common than hematoma and would likely present with more dramatic initial findings. Acute rejection is less likely to present so acutely post-biopsy as the primary event without other preceding indicators.

<step 7> Given the rapid deterioration in renal function and the presence of an expanding perinephric hematoma, the diagnosis is clear. The elevated blood pressure is a secondary consequence of the AKI and the hematoma. The primary problem requiring immediate attention and management is the perinephric hematoma and its impact on the transplanted kidney function.

### Answer:
Acute kidney injury secondary to perinephric hematoma post-renal transplant biopsy.

reasoning_eval.step: The patient is a 70-year-old male with a history of kidney transplant who presented with acute kidney injury (AKI) and elevated blood pressure following a renal transplant biopsy. This temporal relationship strongly suggests that the biopsy procedure is the inciting event for the current acute deterioration.

Post-biopsy, the patient developed a perinephric hematoma, confirmed by ultrasound. This hematoma is a significant complication of renal biopsy. Perinephric hematomas can cause AKI through several mechanisms, including direct compression of the renal parenchyma, compression of the renal vasculature leading to reduced renal blood flow and ischemia, and obstruction of the collecting system if large enough (though less likely in this context based on description, more likely due to compression).

The patient's clinical course is consistent with a perinephric hematoma causing AKI.  He developed oliguria (reduced urine output), a rapid rise in serum creatinine (indicating worsening renal function), and a decrease in hemoglobin (consistent with blood loss into the hematoma). The hematoma was also noted to be expanding, further exacerbating the renal insult.

The elevated blood pressure post-biopsy is also likely related to the acute kidney injury and the perinephric hematoma.  Reduced renal perfusion due to compression from the hematoma can activate the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system (RAAS), leading to sodium and water retention and increased blood pressure.  Pre-existing hypertension makes him more susceptible to blood pressure fluctuations.

While the patient has a complex past medical history including hypertension, diabetes, and previous renal cell carcinoma and hypertensive nephropathy leading to ESRD, these are chronic conditions. The acute nature of the presentation, directly following the renal biopsy and coinciding with hematoma formation, points overwhelmingly to a post-biopsy complication as the primary diagnosis for the current AKI.  Immunosuppression regimen is important in transplant patients, but less likely to be the direct cause of this acute event compared to the procedural complication.

Considering the clinical picture, the most likely diagnosis is acute kidney injury secondary to perinephric hematoma post-renal transplant biopsy.  While other causes of AKI in a transplant recipient should be considered in a broader differential (like rejection, infection, drug toxicity), the acute onset immediately following the biopsy, the presence of a hematoma, and the supportive clinical and laboratory findings strongly favor this diagnosis. Other less likely possibilities, such as renal artery thrombosis or severe vascular injury during biopsy, could theoretically contribute but are less common than hematoma and would likely present with more dramatic initial findings. Acute rejection is less likely to present so acutely post-biopsy as the primary event without other preceding indicators.

Given the rapid deterioration in renal function and the presence of an expanding perinephric hematoma, the diagnosis is clear. The elevated blood pressure is a secondary consequence of the AKI and the hematoma. The primary problem requiring immediate attention and management is the perinephric hematoma and its impact on the transplanted kidney function.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: Step 1: Acute kidney injury (AKI) developed due to rising creatinine levels and reduced urine output following a procedure, with no signs of prerenal azotemia, obstruction, or intrinsic renal disease; symptoms were attributed to external renal compression from a perinephric hematoma.

Step 2: An expanding perinephric hematoma caused extrinsic compression of the transplanted kidney, leading to activation of the RAAS, hypertension, and worsening kidney function; oliguria and elevated blood pressure further supported this diagnosis; imaging confirmed the presence of a hematoma.

Step 3: Chronic rejection or transplant glomerulopathy was considered but excluded due to the temporal relationship between the biopsy and acute symptom onset, suggesting a mechanical cause (hematoma) rather than chronic immunologic rejection.
Step 4: Bleeding diathesis due to aspirin use was identified as a possible contributing factor for ongoing bleeding post-biopsy.
Step 5: The patient was diagnosed with Page kidney induced by a perinephric hematoma after transplant biopsy, resulting in acute kidney injury and arterial hypertension.
Step 6: Urgent surgical decompression was required to relieve renal compression and restore kidney function.
Step 7: Clinical and biochemical parameters improved following the surgical intervention.
